coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of highly luminescent fluorinated graphene quantum dots with tunable fluorine coverage and size

Hou, Kaiming

Wang, Jinqing
Yang, Shengrong
coredata.dc:description: Synthesizing fluorinated graphene quantum dots (FGQDs) has been a great challenge due to the chemically inert C-F bond, and few attempts have been made to prepare this material. In this article, a novel, mild and effective method to controllably prepare FGQDs with tunable fluorine coverage and size by employing commercial fluorinated graphite is developed. This method avoids the complex procedures of preparing fluorinated graphene and use of toxic gases, and can be realized under mild conditions. Moreover, the obtained FGQDs possess relatively uniform size, enjoy good solubility and stability in water and exhibit highly bright blue luminescence.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photoluminescence
Graphene
Fluorinated graphene quantum dots
Carbon material

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of (a) FG-60 and (b) FG-100; full survey XPS spectra of (c) FG-60 and (d) FG-100. The chemical components of the FG samples are labeled in the spectra.

TEM images of the obtained FGQDs samples: (a) A wide-field image of the obtained FGQDs-60; (b) A closer look at the size and shape of FGQDs-60; (c) Size distribution of FGQDs-60; Images of (d–f) show the corresponding experimental results of FGQDs-100.

Chemical composition analyses of the two samples. (a) XPS survey spectra for FGQDs-60 and FGQDs-100. The chemical components of the two FGQDs samples are labeled; (b) High-resolution XPS spectra of F1s for the two samples; (c, d) High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s for the two samples.

Room temperature PL performance of the FGQDs samples dispersed in water: (a) PL and PLE spectra of FGQDs-60; (b) PL spectra of FGQDs-60 at excitation wavelengths varying from 320 to 420nm; Data of (c–d) show the corresponding experimental results of sample FGQDs-100.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of highly luminescent fluorinated graphene quantum dots with tunable fluorine coverage and size

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Solid Lubrication, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou, 730000, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, 100080, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Synthesizing fluorinated graphene quantum dots (FGQDs) has been a great challenge due to the chemically inert C-F bond, and few attempts have been made to prepare this material. In this article, a novel, mild and effective method to controllably prepare FGQDs with tunable fluorine coverage and size by employing commercial fluorinated graphite is developed. This method avoids the complex procedures of preparing fluorinated graphene and use of toxic gases, and can be realized under mild conditions. Moreover, the obtained FGQDs possess relatively uniform size, enjoy good solubility and stability in water and exhibit highly bright blue luminescence.

ce:section.ce:para: Recent years have witnessed the discovery and prosperity of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) due to its novel and noble properties characteristic of higher fluorescence, finer biocompatibility, lower toxicity and less environment concern [1–3]. What makes it more fascinating and applicable is doping or modifying GQDs with foreign atoms or functional groups to tune intrinsic structure [4], manipulate electronic state [5], or adjust surface environment [6], and so on.

Accordingly, it is eagerly expected what other novel properties can be brought to GQDs if fluorine atoms are introduced into the small islands, because modification of graphene with fluorine has endowed graphene exotic fluorescence [7], special magnetic [8], and intriguing biological function [9], and so on. Thus a facile method to synthesize FGQDs is particularly important for both experimental studies and other potentially valuable applications. However, due to the chemical inertness of C-F bonds and lack of an effective way to synthesize fluorinated graphene (FG), finite method has been realized to produce FGQDs [10].

In this article, we report a facile method to prepare FGQDs with tunable fluorine coverage and size by using commercial fluorinated graphite (FGi) as the raw material, and this method avoids the complex procedures to prepare FG and employment of toxic F2 or XeF2. The obtained FGQDs possess highly uniform size distribution, enjoy good stability in water and exhibit bright blue emission. And more interestingly, few-layered FG sheets with different fluorine contents can also be readily obtained during pre-treating FGi in our method.

Pre-treatment of FGi to prepare FG FGi (0.1g) was added into 15mL ethanediamine (EDA) and the mixture was heated at 60 or 100oC under nitrogen atmosphere for 3h. After cooling down, 50mLN-methyl pyrrolidone was introduced and then the solution was mildly sonicated for 10h. After delamination, the upper liquid was pipetted, followed by filtration and freeze drying. The corresponding samples were denoted as FG-60 and FG-100, respectively.

Preparation of FGQDs 50mg FG-60 was first dispersed in 10mL concentrated H2SO4 and 3mL ultra-pure water, and then was sonicated for 2h (500W, 40kHz). After 30mL concentrated HNO3 was added, the solution was further sonicated for another 20h. Then the mixture was diluted by 250mL water and filtered through a 0.22-µm microporous membrane. After being re-dispersed in 40mL water and tuned to pH=8, the dispersion was heated at 200°C for 10h, followed being filtered through 0.22-µm microporous membrane and dialyzed in a dialysis bag (500Da) for three days. The obtained FGQDs were abbreviated as FGQDs-60. Similarly, FGQDs-100 was prepared from FG-100 and purified under the same conditions.

Characterization The chemical composition and morphology of the samples were investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, EscaLab 250Xi) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai F30, operated at 300kV). The photoluminescence performance was recorded on a FLS-920T fluorescence spectrophotometer equipped with a 450W Xe light source and double excitation monochromators.

Due to the high electronegativity of F and the strong bond energy of C-F bond [11], pure FGi or FG is chemically inert and even stable against the erosion of aqua regia, posing great challenge for directly cutting FG to achieve FGQDs. Meanwhile, it is just the high electronegativity gap between carbon and fluorine atom (a gap value of 1.45) [11] that provides the opportunity for nucleophilic reagents to attack carbon and substitute fluorine on FG. Therefore, in our experimental designing plan, nucleophilic EDA was first introduced and reacted with FGi to substitute some fluorine atoms to activate the inert material and to prepare activated FG with less fluorine. Experimental data demonstrate that FG sheets with different fluorine contents can be obtained and then they can be employed for following preparation of FGQDs.

Fig. 1 shows the TEM images and XPS spectra of the two obtained FG samples. It is clearly observed from Fig. 1(a, b) that large and transparent FG sheets with edges freely covering several pores on the Cu grids can be readily obtained by pre-treating FGi. Chemical component analyses shown in Fig. 1(c, d) reveal that no obvious nitrogen can be detected from XPS investigation, indicating the substituted amidogen is not stable enough on FG. Meanwhile, FG sheets with different fluorine contents can be obtained (for FG-60, the fluorine content is about 31%; while for FG-100, it’s about 18%), both of which are lower than initial FGi (51%), indicating the successful occurrence of nucleophilic reaction process.

A definitive quality identification of the prepared FGQDs was first made by TEM characterization to investigate their morphology. It is clearly seen in Fig. 2(a) that FGQDs-60 with relatively uniform size is successfully obtained, and a closer look at the quantum dots in the magnified TEM image in Fig. 2(b) reveals the average lateral dimension of the sample is about 3.0 nm. Meanwhile, statistic size distribution in Fig. 2(c) shows the size of the FGQDs-60 mostly (about 67%) falls within the scope of 2.5 to 3.5 nm. As for FGQDs-100 (Fig. 2(d–f)), it is noticed that the average size of FGQDs-100 is about 2.5 nm and most sizes of the samples (about 65%) are in the range of 2.0 to 3.0nm, which is smaller than that of FGQDs-60. It seems that under the same reaction conditions, FG-100 with less fluorine contents is softer and thus it tends to be more readily cut than FG-60.

To present clearer element information of the samples, XPS measurements were carried out on the FGQDs. XPS survey spectra in Fig. 3(a) reveal that only carbon, oxygen and fluorine elements can be detected on the two FGQDs, indicating the high purity of the samples. As shown in Fig. 3(b), the F1s spectra of FGQDs located at 689.0eV are a reflection of covalent C-F bonds on a carbon structure [12]. A more visualized composition and nature of the chemical bonds of the samples were investigated by high-resolution XPS spectra and provided in Fig. 3(c, d). Three fitted peaks characteristic of weak C-F bond, covalent C-F bond and –CF2 bond can be readily found on the two samples [8]; besides these, C-O groups in the forms of C-O and C=O also exist on the two FGQDs [12].

To have a good understanding on the photoluminescence (PL) properties of the samples, PL and PL excitation (PLE) spectrum experiments were carried out on the samples. FGQDs-60 presents a strong peak at 434 nm when excited by 321 nm wavelength ( Fig. 4(a)), while FGQDs-100 exhibits a similar emission peak at 435 (Fig. 4(c)). Meanwhile, both the PLE spectra of the two samples under the monitor of 434 nm and 435 nm show strong peaks around 320 nm, and accordingly they display similar bright blue emission. On the other hand, excitation-dependent PL behavior is commonly found in fluorescent carbon materials [13,14], and it is observed that both of samples exhibit excitation-dependent PL behaviors when excited from 320 nm to 420 nm (Fig. 4(b, d)). As for FGQDs-60, the emission peaks vary from 492nm (excited by 420 nm) to 434 nm (excited by 320 nm); while for FGQDs-100 the emission peaks shift from 490 nm (excited by 420 nm) to 435 nm (excited by 320 nm). PL research and experimental data analyses of the two FGQDs indicate that although the size and chemical composition of the two samples vary from each other, they exhibit very similar PL performance; the excitation-dependent performance of the two FGQDs that affords multi-PL colors under different excitation wavelengths is potentially important for certain practical applications.

In summary, a mild, operationally simple and controllable method is developed to synthesize FGQDs from FGi. Experimental investigations concerning on morphology, chemical components, and PL performance indicate that highly fluorescent FGQDs with tunable size and fluorine coverage can be readily obtained from commercialized FGi, and the obtained FGQDs possess uniform size, enjoy good water solubility and exhibit excitation-dependent PL behaviors. Considering the easily available raw materials, mild reaction conditions, and the unique properties of the FGQDs samples, this work will provide enlightening insights into chemical modification and preparation of GQDs, as well as promote more research enthusiasm and interests into FGQDs and other carbon-based materials.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank Professor Yuhua Wang and Dr. Quansheng Wu in Lanzhou University for PL analyses, and the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 51375474 and 51205385) and the “Funds for Distinguished Young Scientists of Gansu Province.”

sb:title.sb:maintitle: CRC Handbook of chemistry and physics: a ready reference book of chemical and physical data
